Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study for a few hours each day, but it rarely worked out that way. I'd end up cramming a lot of hours into random bursts, especially when anxiety hit about falling behind. Sometimes I'd sit with my headphones on listening to lecture recordings while doing other things, which felt productive but probably wasn't always. I put in around 31 hours a week but it was scattered and unfocused most of the time.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd have good intentions at the start of each week, but then life would get in the way—work, social stuff, or just not feeling mentally up to it. Deadlines were really the only thing that forced me into action. I'd go days without touching anything, then suddenly spend an entire night trying to catch up. It was exhausting and I knew it wasn't sustainable, but I couldn't seem to break the cycle.
Q3: Usually it was panic, to be honest. I'd realize an assignment was due soon or I'd get an email notification about something I'd missed. Sometimes I logged in just to check if there was anything urgent, scrolled around for a bit, and then logged out without really doing much. It wasn't like I had a habit of checking in regularly—it was more reactive than proactive.
Q4: Not really, no. I tried a few times to make it a daily thing, like checking the LMS with my morning coffee, but it never stuck. The interface felt overwhelming with all those notifications about things I was behind on, which just spiked my anxiety. So instead of it becoming routine, it became something I avoided until I absolutely couldn't anymore.
Q5: The audio recordings and video lectures were my lifeline since I learn better by listening. I could put them on while walking or doing chores, which made them feel less like a burden. Discussion posts were occasionally helpful when other students explained things in simpler terms than the instructor. But honestly, I didn't use most resources to their full potential.
Q6: I basically ignored the reading materials and most of the supplementary PDFs. They felt dense and time-consuming, and I'd tell myself I'd get to them later but never did. The discussion forums I only checked sporadically—posting ten times over the whole course isn't much. I think I was just too overwhelmed to engage with anything that wasn't absolutely essential.
Q7: If I could see an immediate connection to passing an exam or completing an assignment, it felt worth it. Activities that seemed vague or optional got pushed aside because I couldn't justify the mental energy. With my anxiety levels, I had to be really selective about where I put my focus. If something didn't have a clear payoff, I just couldn't bring myself to prioritize it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. When you're barely keeping your head above water with the required stuff, anything labeled 'optional' automatically goes to the bottom of the list. Required activities had this weight to them—like a constant pressure—while optional ones were easy to dismiss. I know I probably missed out on deeper learning, but survival mode doesn't leave room for extras.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I downloaded a lot of the audio lectures and listened to them on my phone when I wasn't near my computer. I also used some YouTube videos and external podcasts that explained concepts in different ways. Sometimes I'd study from notes I'd written out by hand, which helped me process things auditorily by reading them aloud to myself. None of that would show up in any system.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was genuinely engaging with course material but the LMS wouldn't know. Like, I'd download a lecture once and listen to it three or four times over the week. Or I'd be talking through concepts with a friend from class over coffee. I also did a lot of thinking and processing while doing mundane tasks—the learning was happening, just not in a trackable way.
Q11: All the time. I'd grab the audio files or download the slides once, then work with them offline for days. My actual engagement with that material was way higher than my login frequency would suggest. It was easier for me to focus without the LMS interface staring at me with all its notifications and deadlines. Offline felt safer somehow.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when everything piled up. My anxiety was through the roof, and I'd freeze up instead of taking action. I had some personal stuff going on too—nothing major, but enough to drain my energy. The mindfulness exercises they suggested helped a little, but honestly, when you're that stressed, it's hard to even remember to breathe properly.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired or stressed, the LMS became this thing I dreaded opening. Competing responsibilities from my part-time work and extracurriculars meant the course often got deprioritized. Confusion about assignments made me avoid them rather than seek clarification—I know that's backwards, but anxiety does weird things to your brain. I'd end up doing the bare minimum just to get by.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. Or I'd scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing anything. It was like going through the motions to feel like I was doing something productive, but I wasn't actually learning. My task completion was around 60% but my real engagement was probably lower.
Q15: Constantly. I'd know an assignment was important but the anxiety about not doing it well enough would paralyze me. So I'd avoid it, which made the anxiety worse, which made me avoid it more. It's a terrible cycle. I'd find myself doing literally anything else—cleaning my room, organizing files—just to avoid facing the thing I was scared of messing up.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd ignore something for weeks, then scramble at the last minute. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically right before due dates. It's not a healthy way to work, and the quality of what I submitted definitely suffered. But without that external pressure, I just couldn't seem to motivate myself.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it did affect me, but I often avoided looking at it right away because I was scared of what it might say. Positive feedback gave me a temporary boost, but negative feedback would send me spiraling. I think I checked feedback less often than I should have because of that fear. It's like I wanted to know but also really didn't.
Q18: The structure felt both helpful and overwhelming. Having everything laid out meant I knew what was expected, but seeing all those modules and deadlines at once triggered my anxiety. I would've done better with smaller, more frequent check-ins rather than big assignments. The weekly format made sense in theory, but I couldn't keep up with it consistently.
Q19: The fact that so much was online made it easier to avoid. If I had to show up somewhere physically, I might have been more accountable. The flexibility was supposed to help, but for someone like me, it just meant more opportunities to procrastinate. Clearer audio instructions would have helped since that's how I learn best. The written walls of text were intimidating.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me, actually. You might log in a bunch of times, watch some videos, browse around, but never actually complete the assignments because you're stuck in avoidance mode. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes being in the LMS is just a way of feeling like you're doing something when you're really just anxious and paralyzed.
Q21: That definitely resonates with me. My attendance was pretty low and my LMS engagement was minimal, but I still passed with decent exam scores. I did a lot of learning offline—listening to recordings, talking things through, reading my notes aloud. The system would see me as disengaged, but I was engaging, just not in ways that got tracked. Different learning styles matter.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked on things and how long you maybe had them open, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention or understanding anything. They miss all the offline work, the conversations with classmates, the mental processing that happens away from the screen. They also can't capture why someone might be struggling—like anxiety or personal issues—that affect engagement.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to pass. It's when you're curious and want to dig deeper, when you think about concepts outside of class time. I don't think I was truly engaged most of this course—I was in survival mode, doing what I had to do to get through. Real engagement feels different, more alive.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety affected everything I did or didn't do. It wouldn't show the hours I spent worrying about assignments instead of doing them, or the offline studying that actually helped me pass my exams. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness exercises I tried or the conversations with friends that helped me understand concepts. There's a whole invisible layer to my experience that data just can't see.
